topics.title: The Minimum Ethical AI Release Checklist (That Teams Actually Use)
topics.dek: A concise pre-release checklist for AI features: ownership, evidence, user impact, and rollback readiness.

sections.heading: Keep it release-shaped, not policy-shaped
paragraphs: Most ethics checklists fail because they are too abstract for release day. Start with what your team already does before shipping: go/no-go owner, test evidence, incident path, and customer communication notes.
If an item cannot be answered with a link to a ticket, run log, or decision record, rewrite it. The best checklist feels boring and operational.

sections.heading: Six checks to require every time
paragraphs: 1) Named accountable owner. 2) Model/deployment version recorded. 3) Known high-risk failure modes reviewed. 4) Safety and misuse tests run. 5) Escalation and rollback owner assigned. 6) User-facing limitations documented.
These are intentionally small. They create a baseline that can be audited and repeated across releases.

sections.heading: How to avoid checklist theater
paragraphs: Treat every 'No' as a tracked risk decision, not a silent exception. Require an expiry date and owner for accepted risk.
If a checklist item gets auto-approved three releases in a row, either automate evidence collection or remove the item.

topics.title: An Ethical AI Risk Register Template You Can Maintain in 15 Minutes
topics.dek: A lightweight register design for teams that need traceability without adding heavy governance overhead.

sections.heading: What to track (and what to ignore)
paragraphs: Track risks that can change a release decision: harmful outputs, unfair outcomes, privacy leakage, unsafe tool actions, and misuse patterns. Ignore broad philosophical notes that cannot be actioned in sprint planning.
For each risk, record: trigger, impact, current controls, residual risk, owner, and next review date.

sections.heading: Cadence that does not collapse
paragraphs: Update the register in three moments only: before release, after incidents, and when model/data context changes. This keeps it alive without creating a weekly ceremony people skip.
Pin one KPI to the register health: percent of risks with an owner and next review date.

sections.heading: Use incidents as input, not blame
paragraphs: When something goes wrong, add a new row and one control update. Do not rewrite the whole framework.
This creates an institutional memory that survives team turnover and helps future approvals move faster.

topics.title: Human Override Rules for AI Systems: Who Can Stop What, and When
topics.dek: Define clear override authority and escalation paths before production pressure makes decisions for you.

sections.heading: Every AI workflow needs a brake pedal
paragraphs: Ethical AI frameworks are incomplete without explicit override authority. If nobody knows who can disable a model behavior during an incident, your governance exists only on slides.
Write override rules in role language: Support lead can disable feature flag X, Incident commander can block tool Y, Product owner can restrict geography Z.

sections.heading: Design the escalation ladder
paragraphs: Document three levels: immediate containment (minutes), temporary mitigations (hours), and long-term corrective actions (days). Assign owners and communication channels for each level.
Include customer-facing messaging ownership so public comms do not lag engineering decisions.

sections.heading: Practice before you need it
paragraphs: Run one tabletop exercise per quarter on a realistic failure mode. Measure decision latency and handoff clarity, not just technical fixes.
Teams that rehearse overrides recover faster and make fewer ad-hoc policy decisions under pressure.
